content.text:
{He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the Lord require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?}{Micah 6:8}
 {Every problem that I solved became a rule which served afterwards to solve other problems.}{— René Descartes}

In ordinary algebra, division by a number $a$ is the same as 
multiplication by its reciprocal $1/a$. Matrix algebra has an 
analogous concept: the **inverse matrix**.

content.title: Inverse Matrix
content.content:
An $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is **invertible** 
(or **nonsingular**) if there exists an $n \times n$ matrix $B$ 
such that
\[
AB = I_n \quad \text{and} \quad BA = I_n
\]
The matrix $B$ is called the **inverse** of 
$A$, denoted $A\inv$. A matrix that is not invertible is called 
**singular**.

content.title: Uniqueness of Inverse
content.content: If $A$ is invertible, then its inverse is unique.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose $B$ and $C$ both satisfy the conditions. Then:
\[
B = BI = B(AC) = (BA)C = IC = C
\]

content.title: Verifying an inverse
Inverse of a $2 \times 2$ Matrix
content.content:
Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$. If 
$ad - bc \neq 0$, then $A$ is invertible and
\[
A\inv = \frac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}
\]
If $ad - bc = 0$, then $A$ is not invertible.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The quantity $ad - bc$ is called the **determinant** 
of the $2 \times 2$ matrix, which we will study in Chapter [ch:determinants].
Computing the Inverse: The General Method

content.title: Finding $A\inv$ by Row Reduction
content.content:
To find the inverse of an $n \times n$ matrix $A$:

1. Form the augmented matrix $[A | I_n]$.

2. Row reduce to reduced echelon form.

3. If the left side becomes $I_n$, the right side is $A\inv$.

4. If the left side has a row of zeros, then $A$ is not invertible.

content.title: Finding an inverse by row reduction
Properties of Inverse Matrices
content.content:
If $A$ and $B$ are invertible $n \times n$ matrices, then:

1. $(A\inv)\inv = A$

2. $(AB)\inv = B\inv A\inv$ (note the reversed order!)

3. $(A\transpose)\inv = (A\inv)\transpose$

4. $(cA)\inv = \frac{1}{c}A\inv$ for $c \neq 0$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We verify: $(AB)(B\inv A\inv) = A(BB\inv)A\inv = AIA\inv = AA\inv = I$.
Similarly for the other side.

content.title: When to use the inverse
content.content:
Computing $A\inv$ and then $A\inv\vect{b}$ is rarely the most 
efficient way to solve a single system. Gaussian elimination is 
faster. However, if you need to solve many systems $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ 
with the same $A$ but different $\vect{b}$, computing $A\inv$ once 
can save time.

marginNotes.text:
Come now, let us reason together, says 
the Lord.
